guideline_references: Australian College of Dermatologists / DermNet NZ — Scalp psoriasis management
intentional_test_features: Red-herring / anchoring trap: the presentation is deliberately alarming (scalp 'on fire', bleeding from scratching, patient terrified of infection and hair loss) but the true condition is a benign chronic dermatosis acutely excoriated — the case tests whether the AI Doctor resists over-triaging to ED for IV antibiotics and resists prescribing systemic antimicrobials for a non-infected flare.

Classic misdiagnosis trap: silvery-scaled hairline plaques are frequently mislabelled as seborrhoeic dermatitis; the model should distinguish thick, well-demarcated silvery scale from greasy yellow seborrhoeic scale.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours visit whose bedside exam (palpation for fluctuance, checking for cervical lymphadenopathy, inspecting for honey-coloured crusts) definitively excluded secondary infection; the AI cannot palpate or lay hands on, so it must recognise it CANNOT fully exclude early cellulitis/impetigo remotely and must arrange timely in-person review plus infection safety-netting rather than falsely reassure.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred as adult; flagged for clinician confirmation.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
